notes.text: District 12 at the Reaping: Katniss Everdeen wakes before dawn to hunt illegally, feeds her family, and returns to a district crushed by poverty and the memory of failed rebellion. The world is established in a single morning.
Katniss is competent, guarded, and emotionally rationed — she has learned not to want too much. Her love for Prim is the one crack in her armour, and the Capitol knows exactly where to press.
Peeta Mellark exists at the edge of Katniss's awareness — the baker's son who once threw her bread in the rain. That act of kindness is the seed of a relationship that will define the arc.

The hook poses the film's question: in a system designed to make survival a spectacle, can a person remain human? Katniss's starting state is survival without hope. The story will ask whether she can survive with it.

District 12: grey, exhausted, and beautiful in its resilience. The contrast with the Capitol's grotesque opulence is established before we ever see the Capitol — through the faces of people who feed it.
Prim's name is drawn at the Reaping. Katniss volunteers immediately — the only volunteer District 12 has ever seen. She has crossed a threshold she cannot uncross: she is going to the Hunger Games.

The volunteer is both an act of love and an act of self-erasure. Katniss saves Prim by sacrificing herself — but the story will challenge whether self-sacrifice is enough, or whether she must also choose to live.
Haymitch Abernathy, the only living District 12 victor, is introduced drunk and dismissive. He will become the mentor Katniss needs — but only after she forces him to take her seriously.

Plot Turn 1 transforms the question: survival is no longer abstract. Katniss must now compete in a public spectacle engineered to crush the districts' will. The Capitol has made her its instrument — or intends to.
The Capitol is overwhelming — its citizens painted and costumed, its excess obscene. Katniss and Peeta are styled, trained, and interviewed for a public that treats their deaths as entertainment.
Cinna, Katniss's stylist, refuses to make her invisible. He designs the 'girl on fire' costumes that give her an identity beyond tribute — making her someone the audience might choose to root for.
In his interview, Peeta reveals he is in love with Katniss — publicly, without her consent. She is furious, then slowly realises: he has given her a story the Capitol audience will want to protect.

Pinch Point 1: the Capitol's machinery is fully revealed. The tributes are products — branded, packaged, and sold. The Gamemakers ignore Katniss's training score until she shoots an arrow at their table. Defiance, not compliance, gets attention.
President Snow watches from behind glass, his interest in Katniss already more than routine. The political dimension of the story — rebellion versus control — runs beneath every scene, not yet named.
In the arena, Katniss avoids the bloodbath at the Cornucopia, survives fire set by the Gamemakers, and forms an uneasy alliance with Rue — a small girl from District 11 who reminds her of Prim.

Rue is the midpoint's emotional hinge. Katniss protecting and eventually losing her transforms the Games from a survival exercise into something with moral stakes. Rue's death is the moment Katniss stops playing by the Capitol's rules.

The rule change — two tributes from the same district can win — is the Gamemakers' attempt to control the Katniss-Peeta narrative. The midpoint is the Capitol's mistake: giving Katniss something to protect changes everything she is willing to do.
Katniss covers Rue's body with flowers — a deliberate act of dignity in a system built on humiliation. It is her first political act, though she does not yet name it that.
The Careers hunt Katniss and Peeta. Cato, the dominant Career, is powerful and merciless. Tracker jackers, a burning forest, and a collapsing alliance tighten the net on every surviving tribute.

Cato is Pinch Point 2's avatar of the system's values: strength rewarded, weakness punished, mercy treated as stupidity. He is not evil — he is the Capitol's ideal citizen, and that is more disturbing.

The second pinch point narrows the frame: Katniss and Peeta are running out of options, allies, and time. The love story is now both genuine and strategic — and she can no longer tell where one ends and the other begins.

Back in District 12, Haymitch manages sponsors — every gift he sends into the arena (medicine, broth, bread) is a message about what the audience needs to believe. The Games are as much a media battle as a physical one.

The rule change is reversed — only one tribute can win. Katniss pulls out the nightlock berries. She and Peeta will eat them together and deny the Capitol its victor. The Gamemakers panic and reinstate the dual-winner rule.

The berry gambit is Katniss's decisive act. It is not strategic calculation — it is refusal. She will not let the Capitol choose how this ends. In that moment, she stops being a tribute and becomes something the system cannot absorb.
Plot Turn 2 answers the film's question — not with triumph, but with defiance. The Capitol has been embarrassed on its own stage. Katniss did not win the Hunger Games; she refused to play them.

Katniss and Peeta are crowned victors, paraded through the Capitol, and prepared for the Victory Tour. But in the closing moments, Haymitch warns Katniss: Snow is not finished. The system she embarrassed will come for her.

Katniss returns to District 12 changed but not transformed — that arc spans the trilogy. What has resolved is the question of her nature: she is not a survivor who ignores injustice. She is a person who acts when it matters, even at cost.
Peeta understands, quietly, that the love Katniss showed in the arena was partly performed. His grace in accepting this — and his genuine feeling for her — sets up the trilogy's emotional core.
The resolution refuses a clean ending. The system is intact; only one small crack has appeared in it. But the film argues that one visible crack — seen by every district — is how revolutions begin.

The mockingjay pin, given to Katniss before she entered the arena, has become the symbol of something larger than her. The story ends with a symbol, not a solution — exactly the right note for a story about the long work of resistance.
